topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed accusingly on my monitor. I’d been meticulously tracking project allocations for weeks, a task that usually brought a quiet sense of control. Then I saw the figures for Alex. A small, almost imperceptible dip in my own projected earnings, contrasted sharply by the numbers displayed for the junior analyst. My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow intake of air. I reread the data three times, my fingers trembling slightly as I confirmed the discrepancy. It wasn't a mistake; it was a tangible gap in my understanding of the internal structure. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the beige wall opposite. A strange tightness coiled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I decided I needed coffee, a strong, scalding kind, to clear the fog.

[story 2]

"Just checking the quarterly reports," I mumbled to myself, my gaze darting between the screen and the framed motivational poster on the cubicle wall. The numbers flashed up, and the reality hit me with the force of a physical blow. The disparity between my compensation and that of Sarah, who was barely a year out of training, was stark. A cold sweat broke out along my hairline, prickling my skin. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white. I forced a smile when my supervisor walked by, a brittle, unnatural thing. Every casual interaction felt weighted, every shared glance a potential interrogation. I started tapping my pen against the desk rhythmically, a nervous tic I hadn't realized I possessed. The silence of the office suddenly amplified, each ambient hum sounding like a judgment.

[story 3]

I spent the afternoon staring at the metrics, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead seeming unbearably loud. The realization that someone else was accumulating more financial reward for the same output created a strange internal dissonance. My jaw felt clenched so tightly I could taste copper. I started re-reading old performance reviews, searching for any hidden metric, any justification for this imbalance. When I finally looked up, my eyes were slightly unfocused, tracing patterns on the carpet. I felt a profound sense of disorientation, as if the ground beneath my feet had shifted. I walked over to the water cooler, my movements slow and deliberate, my hand hovering over the cold plastic. I took a long, slow sip, trying to anchor myself, but the internal tremor persisted, a low, vibrating anxiety beneath my ribs.
